questions.question: What are the main components of a complete fault-tolerant quantum computation?

options.text: Only error correction and measurement
State preparation, continuous QEC, gate execution, and measurement
Just logical gates and physical qubits
Syndrome extraction only

questions.explanation: A complete fault-tolerant computation requires: (1) fault-tolerant state preparation to initialize logical qubits, (2) continuous QEC cycles to correct errors, (3) fault-tolerant gate execution interleaved with QEC, and (4) fault-tolerant measurement to extract results.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Pauli frame tracking?

options.text: To apply physical corrections to qubits
To classically track corrections without applying physical gates
To measure syndromes faster
To encode logical qubits

questions.explanation: Pauli frame tracking classically records what corrections would have been applied without physically applying them. This is faster, introduces no new errors, and only requires updating classical bits. The frame is resolved at measurement time.

questions.question: In fault-tolerant state preparation of |0_L⟩, why are syndrome measurements repeated d times?

options.text: To make the encoding faster
To verify the state is correctly prepared and detect preparation errors
To apply the encoding circuit
To measure the logical qubit value

questions.explanation: Repeated syndrome measurements verify that the encoded state is correctly prepared. If syndromes are consistently zero for d rounds, we have high confidence that no errors occurred during preparation. Any non-zero syndrome triggers a discard and restart.

questions.question: How does the Pauli frame transform when a Hadamard gate is applied?

options.text: X and Z frames are unchanged
X and Z frames are swapped (X ↔ Z)
Both X and Z frames are cleared
The frame is doubled

questions.explanation: Under conjugation by Hadamard, X → Z and Z → X. Therefore, if the Pauli frame has an X correction pending, it becomes a Z correction after H is applied, and vice versa. This is because H P H† transforms Paulis.

questions.question: What is the main timing constraint for real-time decoding?

options.text: Decoding must finish before the qubit decoheres
Decoding must complete before the next decision point (gate or measurement)
Decoding can take any amount of time
Decoding must be faster than syndrome measurement

questions.explanation: The decoder must produce corrections before the next gate or measurement where those corrections matter. If decoding is too slow, errors accumulate faster than they're corrected, breaking fault tolerance.

questions.question: In the QEC cycle, what happens between logical gate operations?

options.text: Nothing, qubits are idle
Multiple rounds of syndrome extraction and correction
State preparation
Physical qubit reset

questions.explanation: Between logical gates, multiple syndrome rounds (typically d rounds for distance-d codes) are performed to detect and correct any errors that occurred. This continuous QEC prevents error accumulation during computation.

questions.question: At measurement time, a pending X correction in the Pauli frame flips the measured outcome in the Z basis, while a pending Z correction has no effect.
questions.explanation: True. When measuring in the Z basis, the X Pauli operator flips the eigenvalue (|0⟩ ↔ |1⟩), so a pending X correction must flip the measured outcome. The Z operator leaves Z eigenstates unchanged, so pending Z corrections don't affect Z-basis measurement outcomes.
